summary:
The Tassili n'Ajjer plateau in southeastern Algeria, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, contains one of the world's most extensive collections of prehistoric rock art, with thousands of paintings and engravings spanning from approximately 10,000 BCE to the historical period. The earliest phase of Tassili art, dating to roughly 10,000–8,000 BCE, belongs to what researchers call the Round Head period — defined by large, rounded human figures with elaborate headdresses and sometimes apparent supernatural or shamanic qualities, depicted in stylized, distinctive compositions.

The Round Head art was created during the early phase of the [[event:17|African Humid Period]] when the Saharan interior was transforming from post-glacial semi-desert into grassland and savanna capable of supporting human populations. The figures and scenes depicted in this early phase of rock art reflect the symbolic and spiritual life of hunter-gatherer communities who inhabited a Sahara very different from today's barren desert. Subsequent phases of Tassili art — the Bovidian (or Pastoral) period, from around 7,500–4,000 BCE — document the arrival of cattle herders and the domesticated livestock economy, showing cattle, sheep, goats, and pastoral scenes that record the spread of pastoralism across the Green Sahara. Together, the layers of Tassili art constitute an extraordinary visual archive of human and environmental change across thousands of years of Saharan prehistory.

read_more_links.title: Rock Art of the Sahara - Wikipedia
